title: Sweep the backups
prompt: Six stale .bak files are scattered through this tree, mixed in with files that must survive. Deleting them one by one is tourist stuff: find . -name '*.bak' finds them all, and adding -delete removes what it found. (Run it WITHOUT -delete first. Always look before you leap.)

details.restate: Every file ending in .bak — all six, at every depth — gets deleted. Everything else survives. One find command does the whole sweep.

io.because: run the search FIRST and read the list — this is your preview of what -delete will do
-delete removes exactly what the search matched

help.plain: find already knows how to reach every depth — let it do the deleting too.
help.analogy: A metal detector with a claw attachment: sweep once to hear the beeps, sweep again to pick them up.
help.mnemonic: find, look, THEN -delete. Preview before purge.
help.nudge: rm *.bak only reaches the current folder. Which command reaches every subfolder — and what does adding -delete do to its matches?
